I have been thinking about AI agents for twenty years. My PhD at UCD (2010) was on cooperative autonomous intelligent mobile software agents, built using Agent Factory, UCD's multi-agent framework. Twenty-four peer-reviewed papers and a doctoral thesis titled "Competitive Mobile Shopping Using Intelligent Agents." I wrote agent code in 2010 that couldn't really do much in 2010. Sage (sage.botzone.ai): a meeting agent that joins Google Meet via Playwright in a Fargate container, transcribes with Deepgram, classifies utterances with Claude Sonnet, and ships a structured doc (decisions, actions, open questions) sixty seconds after the meeting ends. Cole (cole.botzone.ai): a vision-grounded screen assistant. Watches what a user is doing, listens to their question, tells them where to click. What interests me now: helping founders build durable LLM products on Claude. Eval-first design. results.title: Tanmay Bhopalkar
highlights:
Lead Data Scientist at [Marsh](https://www.linkedin.com/company/marshglobal)
...
I've rebuilt my career twice. On purpose. Civil engineer. Estimation and forecasting in construction. Now Lead Data Scientist at Marsh — where I spend my days inside one of the world's largest risk and insurance firms building the systems that help organizations understand, measure, and govern their exposure. The through-line was never the industry. It was always the same question: what does the data say about what happens next — and who's accountable for it? At Marsh, that question led me deep into cybersecurity GRC. Compliance monitoring, risk quantification, regulatory reporting, control governance — not as abstract concepts, but as working systems inside a large, regulated financial services organization. I built a controls dashboard mapping evidence across 1,000+ applications. A KRI program translating vulnerability exposure across 150,000+ endpoints into something a board could act on. Risk tracking through M&A due diligence. These aren't analytics projects to me — they're risk management problems that happen to require analytical thinking to solve. The regulatory side of that work put me in direct contact with NIST CSF, ISO 27001, GDPR, and NYDFS — building the reports and dashboards that these frameworks require. That experience taught me how they operate in practice inside a large, regulated organization. Now I'm going deeper — learning the architecture behind the frameworks themselves, not just the outputs they generate. The most interesting problems I find right now sit at the intersection of AI and governance. Organizations are deploying AI faster than they can govern it. The gap between what their models do and what their risk and compliance teams understand about them is significant. How do you write a control framework for something that doesn't behave the same way twice? That's the problem I keep coming back to. I share what I'm learning here — about risk, governance, and what it actually looks like to rebuild your career on purpose. Follow if that's interesting to you.
...
